title: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
one_line_summary: This integrated guideline consolidates three prior JCS documents on pulmonary embolism, deep vein thrombosis, and pulmonary hypertension, incorporating 2024 WSPH definitions and providing comprehensive, evidence-based recommendations for prevention, diagnosis, and treatment across the pulmonary circulation.
key_pearls: Pulmonary hypertension (PH) is now defined by right heart catheterization as a mean pulmonary arterial pressure (mPAP) >**20 mmHg**, a change from the previous threshold, but this does not imply a recommendation for therapeutic intervention in patients with mPAP of 21–**24 mmHg**.

The treatment algorithm for idiopathic, heritable, and drug-induced PAH is based on a **3-tier risk stratification model** (low, intermediate, high) using WHO-FC, 6MWD, RAP, CI, and mPAP, with initial oral combination therapy (ERA + PDE5i) for low/intermediate-risk and triple therapy including parenteral prostacyclin for high-risk patients.

Balloon pulmonary angioplasty (BPA) is a Class I recommendation for patients with chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH) who are not candidates for pulmonary endarterectomy (PEA), with evidence showing it can achieve near-normalization of hemodynamics.

For acute pulmonary embolism (PE), treatment strategy is based on severity: high-risk patients in shock require immediate reperfusion therapy, while low-risk patients (sPESI=0, no RV dysfunction) may be candidates for early discharge or outpatient treatment with direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs).

The optimal duration of anticoagulation for venous thromboembolism (VTE) is determined by balancing recurrence risk (provoked vs. unprovoked) against bleeding risk, with extended therapy **recommended** for unprovoked VTE, cancer-associated VTE, and recurrent events.

Inhaled treprostinil is the first pulmonary vasodilator to show efficacy in a randomized controlled trial for pulmonary hypertension associated with interstitial lung disease (ILD-PH), **significantly** improving the 6-minute walk distance.

A new activin signaling inhibitor, sotatercept, has demonstrated **significant** improvement in 6MWD as an add-on therapy for PAH patients not responding adequately to initial combination therapy, representing a potential fourth therapeutic pathway.
For pediatric PAH, risk stratification tools incorporating **BNP/NT-proBNP**, **uric acid**, and imaging are **essential** for prognostication and treatment escalation.

In acute high-risk PE with shock, **systemic thrombolysis** is the first-line reperfusion strategy; surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed therapy are alternatives if thrombolysis is **contraindicated** or fails.
Direct oral anticoagulants (**DOACs**) are preferred over warfarin for extended treatment of VTE in non-cancer patients due to a lower risk of major bleeding.
For cancer-associated VTE, **edoxaban** or **rivaroxaban** are effective; the ONCO DVT trial supports 12-month over 3-month edoxaban for isolated distal DVT.

**Inferior** vena cava (**IVC**) filters are indicated only when anticoagulation is absolutely **contraindicated** in patients with acute proximal DVT or PE, and should be retrieved once the bleeding risk resolves.
Early ambulation and compression stockings are **recommended** for acute DVT to reduce pain and edema, but stockings do not prevent post-thrombotic syndrome.
Inhaled nitric oxide (**iNO**) selectively reduces pulmonary artery pressure in acute massive PE, but its use is limited to hemodynamic stabilization as a bridge to definitive therapy.
consensus_method: Expert consensus based on systematic review of evidence, including proposals from the 6th (2018) and 7th (2024) World Symposia on Pulmonary Hypertension (WSPH), and integration of Japanese-specific data.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Definition, Diagnosis, and Testing of Pulmonary Hypertension
recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- PH is defined as mPAP >**20 mmHg** at rest by RHC (Class I, Level C).
- Precapillary PH is defined as PVR >**2 Wood units** and PAWP ≤**15 mmHg**.
- Exercise PH is defined as an mPAP/CO slope >**3 mmHg/L/min** during exercise (Class I, Level C).
- The diagnostic algorithm involves a stepwise approach from primary care to regional core hospitals and specialized PH centers, with echocardiography as the central non-invasive tool.
- A V/Q scan is the **critical** initial test to rule out CTEPH due to its high sensitivity (Class I, Level C).
- Echocardiographic probability of PH is assessed using peak TRV and additional signs from the ventricles, pulmonary artery, and IVC/RA (Class I, Level B).
- RHC is **essential** for definitive diagnosis, classification, and severity assessment (Class I, Level B).
- Vasoreactivity testing is **recommended** only in idiopathic, heritable, or drug-induced PAH to identify candidates for high-dose calcium channel blockers (Class I, Level C).
- A positive vasoreactivity test is defined as a decrease in mPAP ≥**10 mmHg** to an **absolute** value of ≤**40 mmHg** with stable or increased CO (Class I, Level C).
- Genetic testing and counseling are **recommended** for PAH patients, especially for *BMPR2* mutations (Class I, Level A).
- A new 3-tier JCS risk stratification model for PAH uses five low-risk parameters: WHO-FC I/II, 6MWD >**440 m**, RAP <**8 mmHg**, CI ≥**2.5 L/min/m²**, and mPAP ≤**40 mmHg**.

recommendations.statement: PH is defined as mPAP >**20 mmHg** at rest, measured by right heart catheterization.
It is **recommended** to perform a ventilation–perfusion (V/Q) scan or a lung perfusion scan to assess for CTEPH in patients with PH.
It is **recommended** to perform RHC to confirm the diagnosis, classification, and severity of PH, and to determine and assess the treatment strategy.
It is **recommended** to perform vasoreactivity testing in patients with idiopathic, heritable or drug-induced PAH (I/H/DPAH) to identify candidates for high-dose calcium channel blocker therapy.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Group 1 Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension (PAH) Treatment Algorithm
recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- The treatment algorithm applies to I/HPAH, drug-induced PAH, and SSc-PAH, and is based on a 3-tier risk stratification (low, intermediate, high).
- For patients with a positive acute vasoreactivity test, high-dose calcium channel blockers are **recommended** (Class I, Level A).
- For low- or intermediate-risk patients, initial oral combination therapy with an ERA (ambrisentan or macitentan) and a PDE5 inhibitor (tadalafil) is **recommended** (Class I, Level A).
- For high-risk patients, initial triple combination therapy including a parenteral prostacyclin analog (epoprostenol or treprostinil), an ERA, and a PDE5 inhibitor is **recommended** (Class I, Level B).
- Treatment response should be reassessed within 3-4 months, with hemodynamic evaluation within 6 months if medications are changed (Class I, Level C).
- For intermediate-risk patients on dual oral therapy, adding an oral prostacyclin receptor agonist (selexipag) or inhaled prostacyclin analog (treprostinil) should be considered (Class IIa, Level B).
- For patients with PAH and cardiopulmonary comorbidities, initial monotherapy should be considered (Class IIa, Level C).
- Adding an activin signaling inhibitor (sotatercept) should be considered for patients assessed as intermediate- or high-risk during follow-up (Class IIa, Level B).

recommendations.statement: It is **recommended** to initiate combination therapy with an ERA (ambrisentan or macitentan) and a PDE5 inhibitor (tadalafil) in low- to intermediate-risk patients at the time of diagnosis of IPAH, HPAH, DPAH or SSc-PAH.

It is **recommended** to initiate triple combination therapy consisting of parenteral prostacyclin analogs (epoprostenol or treprostinil), an ERA, and a PDE5 inhibitor in high-risk patients at the time of diagnosis of IPAH, HPAH, DPAH or SSc-PAH.
It should be considered to add an activin signaling inhibitor in patients with IPAH, HPAH, DPAH or SSc-PAH who are assessed as intermediate- or high-risk during follow-up.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Chronic Thromboembolic Pulmonary Hypertension (CTEPH) Diagnosis and Treatment
recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- All patients diagnosed with PH should be evaluated for CTEPH using a V/Q scan (Class I, Level C).
- Treatment strategy must be determined by a multidisciplinary team at a specialized CTEPH/BPA center (Class I, Level C).
- Lifelong anticoagulation with warfarin is **recommended** (Class I, Level C), with DOACs as an alternative (Class IIa, Level B).
- PEA under deep hypothermic circulatory arrest is **recommended** for surgically accessible CTEPH (Class I, Level C).
- BPA is **recommended** for patients who are not candidates for PEA (Class I, Level A).
- Pulmonary vasodilators (riociguat or selexipag) are **recommended** for inoperable CTEPH or residual/recurrent PH after PEA (Class I, Level A).
- Initiating pulmonary vasodilator therapy before BPA should be considered (Class IIa, Level B).
- BPA should be considered for symptomatic patients with CTEPD without PH (Class IIa, Level C).

recommendations.statement: It is **recommended** to perform BPA in patients with CTEPH who are not candidates for surgery.
It is **recommended** to administer pulmonary vasodilators (riociguat or selexipag) in patients with CTEPH who are not candidates for surgery.
It is **recommended** to perform PEA under deep hypothermic circulatory arrest in patients with CTEPH who are eligible for surgery.
It should be considered to use DOACs as an alternative to lifelong vitamin K antagonist therapy in patients with CTEPH.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- Treatment strategy is based on severity, combining hemodynamic parameters, imaging, biomarkers, and clinical scores like sPESI.
- For high-risk PE (cardiac arrest or shock), immediate reperfusion therapy (thrombolysis, surgical or catheter embolectomy) and hemodynamic support (VA-ECMO) are indicated.
- Thrombolytic therapy is **recommended** for patients with acute PE who are in shock or hemodynamically unstable (Class I, Level B).
- For intermediate-high risk PE (RV dysfunction + positive biomarkers), anticoagulation with close monitoring is first-line; rescue reperfusion is considered if deterioration occurs (Class IIa, Level B).
- For low-risk PE (sPESI=0, no RV dysfunction), early discharge or outpatient treatment with DOACs may be considered.
- Anticoagulation with DOACs (edoxaban, rivaroxaban, apixaban) is **recommended** for acute treatment of hemodynamically stable PE (Class I, Level A).

recommendations.statement: Thrombolytic therapy is **recommended** for patients with acute PE who are in shock or hemodynamically unstable due to prolonged hypotension.
DOACs are administered as acute treatment for hemodynamically stable acute PE.
For PE provoked by major transient risk factors or minor transient risk factors (without additional risk factors for recurrence), anticoagulation therapy for 3 months should be considered.
For unprovoked PE, if the bleeding risk is not high, extended anticoagulant therapy should be considered.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- VTE risk in surgical patients is categorized into 4 levels (low, moderate, high, highest) based on procedure type, age, and additional risk factors.
- Early mobilization and active exercise are **recommended** for all at-risk patients (Class I, Level C).
- For high-risk patients, IPC or anticoagulation therapy is **recommended** (Class I, Level A).
- For the highest-risk patients, pharmacologic prophylaxis combined with IPC or elastic stockings is **recommended** (Class I, Level A).
- In medical inpatients, early mobilization is **recommended** for all (Class I, Level C), and IPC is **recommended** for acute stroke patients with reduced mobility (Class I, Level A).
- Anticoagulation therapy should be considered for patients with moderate to severe COVID-19 (requiring oxygen) (Class IIa, Level B).

recommendations.statement: For high-risk patients, intermittent pneumatic compression or anticoagulation therapy is **recommended**.
For patients at highest risk, pharmacologic prophylaxis and intermittent pneumatic compression are used or pharmacologic prophylaxis and compression stockings are used.
Intermittent pneumatic compression is performed on acute stroke patients with reduced mobility.
Consider anticoagulant therapy for the prevention of thrombosis and improvement of the condition in patients with moderate to severe COVID-19 disease (requiring oxygen administration).

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- Genetic testing for **BMPR2** and **ALK1** mutations informs **prognosis** in childhood PAH.
- Risk stratification tools in pediatric PAH incorporate **BNP/NT-proBNP** levels, **uric acid**, and echocardiographic parameters.
- The TOPP Registry provides current clinical practice data on acute vasodilator response testing in children.

recommendations.statement: Risk stratification using multimodal tools including biomarkers and imaging is **recommended** for pediatric PAH patients to guide therapy.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- **Sildenafil**: STARTS-1 and STARTS-2 trials showed efficacy but the FDA issued a warning against its use in pediatric PAH, particularly at high doses.
- **Tadalafil**: A phase 3 RCT demonstrated efficacy and safety in a pediatric PAH population.
- **Bosentan**: FUTURE-1, -2, and -3 studies established pharmacokinetics, safety, and dosing regimens for the pediatric formulation.
- **Ambrisentan**: RCT data supports safety and efficacy in children aged 8-18 years.
- **Macitentan**: Evaluated in patients with Eisenmenger syndrome; real-world data supports safety in pediatric PAH.
- **Prostanoids**: Parenteral prostanoids should be started early, dosed high, and combined. Epoprostenol, treprostinil (subcutaneous/IV), and inhaled iloprost have demonstrated efficacy.
- **Selexipag**: Multicenter experience and systematic reviews indicate safety and efficacy in children with idiopathic/heritable PAH.
- **Riociguat**: PATENT-CHILD study and population PK modeling support its use in children aged ≥6 years.

recommendations.statement: Parenteral prostanoids should be initiated early and at high doses for children with severe PAH.
Oral endothelin receptor antagonists (bosentan, ambrisentan, macitentan) and PDE5 inhibitors (tadalafil) are treatment options for pediatric PAH, with careful monitoring for adverse effects.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Acute Pulmonary Embolism (PE) Risk Stratification and Outpatient Management
recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- The **Hestia criteria** and **simplified PESI score** can identify low-risk PE patients suitable for outpatient treatment.
- The **HoT-PE** trial and **ONCO PE** companion report support home treatment with rivaroxaban for low-risk PE, including in active cancer patients.
- Early discharge and home treatment with **rivaroxaban** is safe and effective for low-risk PE.

recommendations.statement: Outpatient management or early discharge is **recommended** for patients with acute low-risk PE, using validated clinical decision rules (Hestia, sPESI).

recommendation_blocks.topic: Advanced Therapies for High-Risk and Intermediate-Risk PE
recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- **Systemic Thrombolysis**: Meta-analysis confirms reduced **mortality** in high-risk PE but increases major bleeding and intracranial hemorrhage. Low-dose rt-PA is effective and potentially safer.
- **Catheter-Directed Therapy (CDT)**: Ultrasound-assisted thrombolysis (SEATTLE II, SUNSET sPE) and mechanical thrombectomy (FLARE, EXTRACT-PE, FLASH registry) are options for intermediate-risk PE.
- **Surgical Embolectomy**: Multi-institutional studies show acceptable outcomes for acute massive and submassive PE, particularly as first-line therapy in experienced centers.
- **VA-ECMO**: Meta-analyses support its use as a bridge to definitive reperfusion (thrombolysis or embolectomy) in massive PE with cardiac arrest or refractory shock. Systemic thrombolysis during ECMO may improve outcomes.

recommendations.statement: Systemic thrombolysis is **recommended** for acute high-risk PE with hemodynamic instability.

Catheter-directed therapies or surgical embolectomy should be considered for intermediate-risk PE with evidence of right ventricular dysfunction and deterioration, or when thrombolysis is **contraindicated**.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Anticoagulation Duration and Agents for VTE
recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- **Extended anticoagulation** beyond 3-6 months reduces recurrence risk in unprovoked VTE but increases bleeding risk.
- **DOACs** (rivaroxaban, apixaban, edoxaban) are preferred over warfarin for extended treatment due to a better safety profile.
- **Aspirin** is **inferior** to anticoagulation for extended secondary prevention but may be considered if anticoagulation is stopped.
- The **VTE-BLEED score** predicts major bleeding during prolonged anticoagulation.

recommendations.statement: Extended anticoagulation with a DOAC is **recommended** over warfarin for secondary prevention in patients with unprovoked VTE and low bleeding risk.
For patients who stop anticoagulation, low-dose aspirin may be considered for extended prevention of recurrent VTE.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- The **PREPIC** and **PREPIC 2** trials showed IVC filters reduce short-term PE risk but increase long-term DVT risk without a **mortality** benefit.
- Guidelines from SIR, ACC, AHA, and CHEST recommend IVC filters only when anticoagulation is **contraindicated** in patients with acute VTE.
- Retrievable filters should be removed once the contraindication to anticoagulation resolves.

recommendations.statement: IVC filter placement is **recommended** for patients with acute proximal DVT or PE and an **absolute** contraindication to anticoagulation.
Routine use of IVC filters in addition to anticoagulation is not **recommended**.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Compression Therapy and Early Ambulation for DVT
recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- Meta-analyses show **early ambulation** does not increase the risk of PE and reduces acute leg pain and edema.
- **Graduated compression stockings (GCS)** reduce acute symptoms but the **SOX trial** and **OCTAVIA study** showed they do not prevent post-thrombotic syndrome (PTS).
- Individualized duration of compression therapy (IDEAL DVT) is non-**inferior** to standard 24-month therapy for PTS prevention.

recommendations.statement: Early ambulation is **recommended** over bed rest in patients with acute DVT.
Compression stockings may be used to relieve acute DVT symptoms but are not routinely **recommended** for the prevention of post-thrombotic syndrome.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- The **Khorana score** predicts chemotherapy-associated thrombosis; the **ONCO DVT trial** showed 12-month edoxaban is **superior** to 3-month for isolated distal DVT in cancer patients.
- **DOACs** (rivaroxaban, edoxaban) are effective for CA-VTE treatment; the **SELECT-D** and **Hokusai-VTE Cancer** trials inform practice.
- Primary thromboprophylaxis with DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban) in high-risk ambulatory cancer patients (Khorana score ≥2) reduces VTE but increases bleeding.

recommendations.statement: DOACs (edoxaban or rivaroxaban) are **recommended** for the treatment of cancer-associated VTE in patients without high bleeding risk.
Primary thromboprophylaxis with a DOAC may be considered in high-risk ambulatory cancer patients with a Khorana score ≥2 and low bleeding risk.

recommendation_blocks.topic: VTE Prophylaxis in Hospitalized and Surgical Patients
recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- **Caprini** and **Padua** risk assessment models identify high-risk surgical and medical patients.
- **Pharmacologic prophylaxis** (LMWH, fondaparinux, DOACs) reduces VTE in high-risk groups; the **IMPROVE** score assesses bleeding risk.
- **Intermittent pneumatic compression (IPC)** is effective for VTE prevention in patients with contraindications to anticoagulation (e.g., stroke, ICU).
- Combined mechanical and pharmacologic prophylaxis is more effective than either alone in very high-risk patients.

recommendations.statement: Risk assessment with validated tools (Caprini/Padua) is **recommended** for all hospitalized patients to guide VTE prophylaxis.
Pharmacologic prophylaxis with LMWH or DOACs is **recommended** for high-risk surgical and medical patients without contraindications.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
- COVID-19 is associated with a high incidence of thrombotic complications, particularly in **critically** ill ICU patients.
- ISTH and CHEST guidelines recommend standard-dose prophylactic anticoagulation for hospitalized patients; therapeutic-dose anticoagulation may benefit non-**critically** ill patients but not **critically** ill patients (REMAP-CAP, ATTACC, ACTIV-4a).
- Japanese CLOT-COVID study and nationwide surveys provide local epidemiological data.

recommendations.statement: Standard prophylactic-dose anticoagulation is **recommended** for hospitalized patients with COVID-19.
Therapeutic-dose anticoagulation may be considered in select non-**critically** ill hospitalized COVID-19 patients with elevated D-dimer.

bedside_protocol.title: Initial Assessment for Suspected PH
bedside_protocol.action: Primary care physician evaluates medical/family history, physical exam, chest X-ray, ECG, BNP/NT-proBNP, and oxygen saturation. If abnormalities are detected, refer to a regional core hospital.

bedside_protocol.title: Non-Invasive Testing at Regional Core Hospital
bedside_protocol.action: Perform echocardiography to estimate PH probability. Conduct pulmonary function tests, arterial blood gas analysis, and chest CT/CTPA to evaluate for lung disease. If PH probability is intermediate/high, refer to a specialized PH center.

bedside_protocol.title: Confirmatory Diagnosis at PH Center
bedside_protocol.action: Perform a V/Q scan to rule out CTEPH. Conduct RHC for definitive hemodynamic diagnosis, classification, and severity assessment. Perform acute vasoreactivity testing in I/H/DPAH patients.

bedside_protocol.title: Acute PE Severity Stratification
bedside_protocol.action: Assess for cardiac arrest or shock (high-risk). If stable, evaluate using sPESI score and assess for RV dysfunction on echocardiography or CT and cardiac biomarkers to classify as intermediate-high, intermediate-low, or low risk.

bedside_protocol.title: Immediate Management of High-Risk PE
bedside_protocol.action: Initiate VA-ECMO if in cardiac arrest. For shock, start anticoagulation and consider immediate reperfusion therapy (systemic thrombolysis, or surgical/catheter embolectomy if contraindications exist). Administer norepinephrine for hypotension.

strengths_limitations:
- Strengths: This is the first comprehensive national guideline to integrate three prior documents on pulmonary circulation into a single, coherent volume, and the first to be issued following the 7th WSPH (2024), incorporating updated hemodynamic definitions and a new risk stratification model. It systematically incorporates high-quality Japanese-led evidence, particularly for BPA in CTEPH.
- Limitations: Many recommendations are based on expert consensus (Level C evidence) due to a lack of randomized controlled trials in specific areas. The guideline notes that some **recommended** drug regimens, such as low-dose DOACs for extended VTE treatment, are not yet covered by health insurance in Japan, limiting their practical application.
- Strengths: Comprehensive, multidisciplinary guideline covering the full spectrum of PH and VTE management from pediatrics to adults, incorporating the latest RCTs and real-world data, with a focus on Japanese clinical practice.
- Limitations: As a narrative guideline, the strength of individual recommendations is not formally graded; some recommendations rely on expert consensus due to limited high-quality evidence in specific subpopulations.

drugs_doses.dose: 1-2 ng/kg/min continuous IV infusion, titrated to effect (typical range at 1 year: 40-60 ng/kg/min)
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Headache, flushing, jaw/limb pain, nausea, diarrhea; high-dose therapy can trigger IgG4-related disease, thyroid dysfunction, pituitary disorders.

drugs_doses.dose: 200 μg twice daily, titrated to max tolerated dose (≤1600 μg twice daily)
drugs_doses.indication: PAH (add-on to dual oral therapy); CTEPH (inoperable or residual PH after PEA)

drugs_doses.dose: 1 mg three times daily, titrated to 2.5 mg three times daily
drugs_doses.indication: CTEPH (inoperable or residual PH after PEA)
PAH (add-on therapy for intermediate/high-risk patients on background therapy)
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Bleeding, worsening telangiectasia, increased hemoglobin, decreased platelet counts.

drugs_doses.dose: 18 μg four times daily, titrated to 54 μg four times daily for PAH; target dose 72 μg four times daily for ILD-PH

drugs_doses.indication: Acute PE (thrombolytic therapy for high-risk patients)
Acute VTE treatment; CTEPH (alternative to warfarin)

drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Bleeding; embryopathy when used during 6-12 weeks of gestation.
